section.title: The Sign against Egypt and Cush
section.sectionRationale: This brief narrative interrupts the oracles against the nations, chapters 13 to 23, with a living parable: for three years Isaiah himself walks naked and barefoot as a prophetic sign, dramatizing in his own body the shame that will come on Egypt and Cush, the very powers Judah was tempted to trust for rescue from Assyria.
section.thesis: Isaiah's own humiliating sign act, three years walking naked and barefoot, dramatizes the coming shame of Egypt and Cush before Assyria, warning Judah that the nations she hopes in for deliverance cannot even save themselves.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the historical setting of Sargon's commander capturing Ashdod; the LORD's command that Isaiah act out the sign in his own body; and the sign's fulfillment as Assyria leads Egypt and Cush away naked and ashamed.

verses.text: Before the year that the chief commander, sent by Sargon king of Assyria, came to Ashdod and attacked and captured it,

units.label: Isaiah's sign in his own body
units.note: naked and barefoot for three years

verses.text: the LORD had already spoken through Isaiah son of Amoz, saying, “Go, remove the sackcloth from your waist and the sandals from your feet.” And Isaiah did so, walking around naked and barefoot.
Then the LORD said, “Just as My servant Isaiah has gone naked and barefoot for three years as a {{owth|sign}} and {{mopheth|omen}} against Egypt and Cush,

units.label: The sign fulfilled: Egypt and Cush led away in shame

verses.text: so the king of Assyria will lead away the captives of Egypt and the exiles of Cush, young and old alike, naked and barefoot, with bared buttocks, to Egypt's shame.
Those who made Cush their hope and Egypt their boast will be dismayed and ashamed.

And on that day the dwellers of this coastland will say, ‘See what has happened to our source of hope, those to whom we fled for help and deliverance from the king of Assyria! How then can we escape?’”

owth.gloss: A sign, a visible token that points beyond itself to a truth or coming event. God frequently gives signs to confirm His word, but here the sign is Isaiah's own body and humiliation, making the prophet himself a living, walking sermon.

mopheth.gloss: A wonder, portent, or omen, often paired with owth, sign, to describe an act or event that startles observers into recognizing God's hand at work. Isaiah's three years of nakedness functioned as exactly this kind of arresting, unforgettable portent.

basic.context: For three years, Isaiah obeyed a shocking command: to walk around naked and barefoot as a living object lesson. This was not madness but prophetic theater, dramatizing what would soon happen to Egypt and Cush, the very nations Judah's leaders wanted to trust for help against Assyria. When Assyria's army did come, it would strip and lead away Egyptian and Cushite captives in the same shameful state Isaiah had already displayed.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical episode, tied to the actual Assyrian capture of Ashdod around 711 BC under Sargon II, and it functions as a warning against a real, contemporary temptation: relying on Egypt and Cush rather than the LORD. It is not a call for believers today to imitate Isaiah's exact actions, but it does model how God sometimes uses costly, embodied obedience to make an unmistakable point.
basic.originalLanguages: Isaiah's ordeal is called both an owth, sign, and a mopheth, omen or wonder, the pairing Scripture often uses for acts too striking to ignore. His humiliation was designed to be remembered and to strip away, in advance, any illusions Judah had about the safety of trusting Egypt and Cush.
basic.typology: Isaiah's willing embrace of shame and exposure as a sign for others' sake foreshadows Christ, who was stripped, mocked, and shamed publicly, not for His own sin but so that His humiliation would become the sign and wonder that saves those who trust in Him rather than in false securities.
basic.christ: Isaiah bore shame in his own body as a sign to warn others away from false hope; Christ bore far greater shame, stripped and exposed on the cross, not merely as a warning but as the actual payment for sin, so that all who look to Him rather than to any earthly power are saved rather than put to shame, Romans 10:11.
basic.god: God is willing to use costly, even humiliating obedience from His servants to make His warnings vivid and unforgettable. He is also relentlessly opposed to His people's false hopes; no earthly power, however impressive, can shelter those who trust it instead of Him.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 18:21 and Isaiah 30:1-5 warn similarly against trusting Egypt rather than the LORD. Hebrews 12:2 speaks of Christ enduring the cross, scorning its shame, echoing this pattern of costly obedience for others' sake. Romans 10:11 promises that none who trust in Him will be put to shame.
basic.oneStory: Isaiah's three years of humiliation warn that every hope but the LORD ends in shame, since even Egypt and Cush cannot save themselves, let alone anyone who trusts in them. This anticipates Christ, who bore the ultimate shame on the cross so that everyone who trusts in Him, rather than in any earthly power, will never be put to shame.

context.literary: A brief narrative interlude within the oracles against the nations, framed by a historical note, verse 1, and structured around the prophetic sign act itself, God's command and Isaiah's obedience, verses 2 to 3, followed by its meaning and fulfillment, verses 4 to 6.
context.historical: Sargon II of Assyria sent his commander, or Tartan, to crush a rebellion at Ashdod around 711 BC, a rebellion in which Egypt likely encouraged Philistine and other regional resistance against Assyria without actually being able to deliver military help; Isaiah's sign act, begun some three years earlier, anticipated this exact humiliation of Egypt and her Cushite allies.
context.canonical: One of several dramatic prophetic sign acts in Scripture, alongside Ezekiel's lying on his side, Jeremiah's yoke, and Hosea's marriage, in which the prophet's own body and life become the message, embodying truths words alone might fail to convey.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn Judah in the most vivid, unmistakable way possible that Egypt and Cush, tempting allies against Assyria, would themselves be humiliated and could not deliver anyone who trusted in them instead of the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific prophetic sign act commanded for a specific historical warning; it does not prescribe that believers today perform similar acts. The abiding principle is that trusting any power other than the LORD for deliverance ends in the same shame that power itself will suffer.

debates.question: Did Isaiah literally walk naked, or does naked here mean stripped of his outer prophet's garment while still minimally clothed?

views.label: Fully unclothed
views.case: The plain reading of the Hebrew and the parallel with the captives' described shame, verse 4, suggests a genuinely humiliating, fully unclothed state maintained for the full three years.

views.label: Stripped of the outer sackcloth robe only
views.case: Some commentators note that naked in Hebrew idiom can describe someone stripped of an outer garment while wearing an undergarment, softening the extremity while preserving the humiliation and social shame intended.

debates.bearing: Either reading preserves the passage's main point: Isaiah bore real, sustained public shame as a costly, embodied warning, and the extremity of his obedience underlines how seriously God wanted Judah to hear the message.

study.originalLanguages: The pairing owth and mopheth, sign and omen, describes an act meant to be so unforgettable that no one could later claim ignorance of the warning. Isaiah's sustained, humiliating obedience turned his own body into exactly this kind of unmistakable testimony against trusting Egypt and Cush.

typology.type: Isaiah's willing shame as a sign for others' warning
typology.fulfillment: Christ's far greater shame, stripped and exposed on the cross, enduring it for the joy set before Him, Hebrews 12:2, so that trust in Him, not in earthly powers, brings salvation rather than shame.

study.christ: Isaiah accepted three years of public humiliation so that Judah would see, in living color, the folly of trusting Egypt and Cush. Christ accepted a far greater and final humiliation, stripped of His garments and shamed publicly at the cross, not merely to warn but to save, bearing the shame that sin deserved so that everyone who believes in Him will never be put to shame, Romans 10:11, Hebrews 12:2.
study.god: God is willing to ask costly, embodied obedience of His servants in order to drive home a truth people would otherwise ignore. He is also unwavering in His opposition to false security: no ally, however strong it looks, can rescue those who trust it instead of Him, and that ally's own eventual shame proves the point.

crossReferences.note: The Assyrian field commander mocks Judah for trusting Egypt, a bruised reed, the same false hope this sign act warns against.
A later oracle making the same point in words that Isaiah 20 makes in action: Egypt's help is worthless shame, not deliverance.
Christ endured the cross, scorning its shame, the ultimate pattern of costly obedience for the sake of others that Isaiah's sign act foreshadows.
Whoever believes in Him will never be put to shame, the gospel's answer to the shame this chapter warns misplaced trust will bring.

study.oneStory: Isaiah's three years of humiliating obedience warned Judah that Egypt and Cush, however impressive, would themselves be led away in shame and could rescue no one. This vivid sign points forward to Christ, who bore the ultimate shame at the cross, not to warn us away from false hopes only, but to become Himself the sure hope in whom no one who trusts Him will ever be put to shame.

quiz.q: What did the LORD command Isaiah to do as a prophetic sign?

options.text: Walk around naked and barefoot for three years.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 state this command and its three-year duration explicitly.

options.text: Build a model of the Assyrian army out of clay.
options.note: Not found in this text; the sign was Isaiah's own body and state, not a model.
This is a different kind of sign found elsewhere in Scripture, not commanded here.

options.text: Wear sackcloth continuously for three years.
options.note: Close, since sackcloth is mentioned, but the command was specifically to remove it, not to wear it.

quiz.q: What was Isaiah's sign meant to warn against?

options.text: Trusting Egypt and Cush for deliverance from Assyria.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 explicitly name this false hope as the sign's target.
Not the subject of this passage; the concern is political trust in Egypt and Cush.

options.text: Trusting in Judah's own military strength.
options.note: Related in spirit to misplaced trust, but the specific warning names Egypt and Cush as the false hope, not Judah's own army.

quiz.q: What actually happened to Egypt and Cush according to the sign's fulfillment?

options.text: Assyria led their captives away naked and barefoot, in humiliating shame.
options.note: Verse 4 describes exactly this outcome, matching Isaiah's own embodied sign.

options.text: They successfully repelled the Assyrian army.
options.note: The opposite; they are led away in defeat and shame.

options.text: They formed a permanent alliance with Assyria.
options.note: Not described in this passage; they are conquered, not allied.

options.text: They were spared entirely due to their wealth.
options.note: The text describes their humiliation, not exemption from judgment.

quiz.q: How does Isaiah's costly sign act point forward to Christ?

options.text: It foreshadows Christ, who bore real shame and humiliation at the cross for others' salvation.
options.note: Hebrews 12:2 speaks of Christ enduring the cross and scorning its shame, echoing this pattern.

options.text: It shows that Jesus never experienced any suffering or shame.
options.note: The opposite is true; Christ's suffering and shame at the cross are central to the gospel.

options.text: It proves that prophets today must perform similar acts.
options.note: The passage describes a specific historical command, not a universal pattern for believers to imitate literally.

options.text: It is unrelated to any New Testament theme.
options.note: There is a real typological connection to Christ's shame and humiliation, even though the passage's primary meaning is historical warning.

questions: Isaiah endured three years of public humiliation out of obedience to God's word. What costly obedience is God asking of you that feels humiliating or hard to explain to others?
Judah was tempted to trust Egypt and Cush rather than the LORD for deliverance. What powerful ally or resource are you tempted to trust instead of God right now?
The very nations Judah hoped in were themselves led away in shame. Where have you placed hope in something that, in the end, could not even save itself?
Isaiah's shame in his own body previews the far greater shame Christ bore at the cross. How does knowing Christ bore shame for you change how you carry your own moments of shame or humiliation?
The coastland dwellers asked in despair, how then can we escape? Where do you need to remember that escape and rescue are found in the LORD alone, not in any earthly power?

translationNotes.note: Naked most likely describes Isaiah stripped of his outer garment of sackcloth, though the term can denote complete nakedness; either reading preserves the intended public shame and humiliation of the sign.
Where BSB prints an em dash before to Egypt's shame, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
The chief commander renders a Hebrew military title, Tartan, an Assyrian office rather than a personal name, referring to Sargon II's field commander who besieged and captured Ashdod.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 20:1-6. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
